name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن صَلْصَـٰلٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ (٢٦)
```

```english
Certainly We created man out of a dry clay [drawn] from an aging mud, (26)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْجَآنَّ خَلَقْنَـٰهُ مِن قَبْلُ مِن نَّارِ ٱلسَّمُومِ (٢٧)
```

```english
and We created the jinn earlier, out of a piercing fire. (27)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنِّى خَـٰلِقٌۢ بَشَرًا مِّن صَلْصَـٰلٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ (٢٨)
```

```english
When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to create a human out of a dry clay [drawn] from an aging mud. (28)
```

```arabic
فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُۥ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِى فَقَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ سَـٰجِدِينَ (٢٩)
```

```english
So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down in prostration before him.’ (29)
```

```arabic
فَسَجَدَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ (٣٠)
```

```english
Thereat the angels prostrated, all of them together, (30)
```

```arabic
إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ مَعَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (٣١)
```

```english
but not Iblis: he refused to be among those who prostrated. (31)
```

```arabic
قَالَ يَـٰٓإِبْلِيسُ مَا لَكَ أَلَّا تَكُونَ مَعَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (٣٢)
```

```english
He said, ‘O Iblis! What kept you from being among those who have prostrated?’ (32)
```

```arabic
قَالَ لَمْ أَكُن لِّأَسْجُدَ لِبَشَرٍ خَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن صَلْصَـٰلٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ (٣٣)
```

```english
Said he, ‘I will not prostrate before a human whom You have created out of a dry clay [drawn] from an aging mud.’ (33)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ (٣٤)
```

```english
He said, ‘Begone hence, for you are indeed an outcast, (34)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ ٱللَّعْنَةَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٣٥)
```

```english
and indeed the curse shall lie on you until the Day of Retribution.’ (35)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ فَأَنظِرْنِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (٣٦)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! Respite me till the day they will be resurrected.’ (36)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ (٣٧)
```

```english
Said He, ‘You are indeed among the reprieved (37)
```

```arabic
إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْوَقْتِ ٱلْمَعْلُومِ (٣٨)
```

```english
until the day of the known time.’ (38)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ بِمَآ أَغْوَيْتَنِى لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٣٩)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them, all (39)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٤٠)
```

```english
except Your dedicated servants among them.’ (40)
```

```arabic
قَالَ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ عَلَىَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ (٤١)
```

```english
He said, ‘This is the path [leading] straight to Me. (41)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ عِبَادِى لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنٌ إِلَّا مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ ٱلْغَاوِينَ (٤٢)
```

```english
Indeed, as for My servants you do not have any authority over them, except the perverse who follow you, (42)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٤٣)
```

```english
and indeed hell is the tryst of them all. (43)
```

```arabic
لَهَا سَبْعَةُ أَبْوَٰبٍ لِّكُلِّ بَابٍ مِّنْهُمْ جُزْءٌ مَّقْسُومٌ (٤٤)
```

```english
It has seven gates, and to each gate belongs a separate portion of them.’ (44)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (٤٥)
```

```english
Indeed the Godwary will be amid gardens and springs. (45)
```

```arabic
ٱدْخُلُوهَا بِسَلَـٰمٍ ءَامِنِينَ (٤٦)
```

```english
‘‘Enter it in peace and safety!’’ (46)
```

```arabic
وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِى صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ إِخْوَٰنًا عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مُّتَقَـٰبِلِينَ (٤٧)
```

```english
We will remove whatever rancour there is in their breasts; [intimate like] brothers, [they will be reclining] on couches, facing one another. (47)
```

```arabic
لَا يَمَسُّهُمْ فِيهَا نَصَبٌ وَمَا هُم مِّنْهَا بِمُخْرَجِينَ (٤٨)
```

```english
Therein neither weariness shall touch them, nor will they [ever] be expelled from it. (48)
```

## (Commentary)

This is the third group of verses presented following what was mentioned at the opening of the Surah regarding the disbelievers' mockery of the Book and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and their suggestion to him of a sign other than the Quran. In this group, Allah, the Glorified, mentions the beginning of the creation of man and the jinn (الجان), His command to the angels and Iblis to prostrate to him, their prostration and the refusal of Iblis—who was of the jinn—his expulsion (رجمه), and his misleading of the children of Adam, as well as what Allah, the Glorified, decreed at that time regarding the felicity of the God-conscious (المتقين) and the wretchedness of the perverted (الغاوين).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ صَلْصالٍ مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ** — "Certainly We created man out of a dry clay (صلصال) [drawn] from an aged mud (حمإ مسنون)." (15:26). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The root of *al-salsal* (الصلصال) is the resonance of sound from a dry object; from it, it is said: 'the nail resonated' (صل المسمار). Dry clay is called *salsal*. Allah says: **مِنْ صَلْصالٍ كَالْفَخَّارِ** — 'from dry clay like pottery' (55:14) and **مِنْ صَلْصالٍ مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ**. *Al-salsalah* (الصلصلة) is also the remains of water, so named because it mimics the sound of its movement in a water-skin. It is also said: *al-salsal* is stinking clay, from their saying: 'the meat has stunk' (صل اللحم)."

He also said: "*Al-ham'ah* (الحمأة) and *al-ham'a* (الحمأ) are black, stinking mud." Regarding His saying: **مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ**, he said: "It is said to mean 'altered' (متغير). And His saying: **لم يتسنه** (2:259) means 'it did not alter,' and the *ha* is for pause (للاستراحة)." [End of quote].

His saying: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ** etc., refers to the beginning of the creation of man, as evidenced by His saying: **وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلالَةٍ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ** — "He began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid." (32:7-8). Thus, it is an account of the creation of the species and its appearance on earth; for the creation of the first of them, from whom the rest were created, is the creation of all.

It is stated in *Majma' al-Bayan*: "The origin of Adam was from dust (تراب), and that is His saying: **خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ** (3:59). Then He made the dust into clay (طين), and that is His saying: **وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ** (7:12). Then He left that clay until it altered and became soft, and that is His saying: **مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ**. Then He left it until it dried, and that is His saying: **مِنْ صَلْصالٍ**. There is no contradiction in these statements, as they are reports of its different states." [End of quote].

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَالْجَانَّ خَلَقْناهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ مِنْ نارِ السَّمُومِ** — "And the jinn (الجان) We created before from a smokeless fire (نار السموم)." (15:27). Al-Raghib said: "*Al-samum* (السموم) is the hot wind that affects like the effect of poison (السم)." [End of quote]. The root of *al-jinn* (الجن) is concealment (الستر), a meaning that permeates all its derivatives, such as *al-jinn* (الجن), *al-mijannah* (المجنة) — *the shield*, *al-jannah* (الجنة) — *the garden/paradise*, *al-janin* (الجنين) — *the embryo*, *al-janan* (الجنان) — *the heart/soul*, and **جن عليه الليل** — "the night enshrouded him," and so on.

The jinn (الجن) are a group of existents naturally concealed from our senses, possessing consciousness (شعور) and will (إرادة). Their mention is repeated in the Noble Quran, and wondrous deeds and rapid movements are attributed to them, as in the stories of Solomon (عليه‌السلام). They are legally responsible (مكلفون), they live, die, and will be resurrected (يحشرون); many scattered verses in His speech indicate all of this.

As for the term *al-jann* (الجان), is it the jinn (الجن) themselves, or is it the progenitor of the jinn just as Adam (عليه‌السلام) is the progenitor of mankind—as narrated from Ibn Abbas? Or is it Iblis himself—as narrated from al-Hasan? Or is *al-jann* the progeny of Iblis from the jinn, or a specific type of jinn as mentioned by al-Raghib? These are differing opinions, most of which lack evidence.

That which contemplation (التدبر) of His speech leads to is that He has contrasted "man" (الإنسان) with "the jinn" (الجان) in these two verses, making them two distinct species that are not without some form of connection in their creation. Similar to this is His saying: **خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ صَلْصالٍ كَالْفَخَّارِ وَخَلَقَ الْجَانَّ مِنْ مارِجٍ مِنْ نارٍ** — "He created man out of a dry clay like pottery, and He created the jinn out of a flame of fire." (55:14-15).

The context of the verses here is not devoid of indication that Iblis was of the jinn (جانا); otherwise, His saying: **وَالْجَانَّ خَلَقْناهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ** etc., would be superfluous. Allah has said in another place regarding Iblis: **كانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ** — "He was one of the jinn, so he transgressed against his Lord’s command." (18:50). This informs us that this mentioned *jann* is the jinn (الجن) itself or a type among the types of jinn. Subsequently, in the rest of His speech, He ceased mentioning *al-jann* and mentioned only *al-jinn*, even in instances where the speech encompasses Iblis and his tribe, such as His saying: **شَياطِينَ الْإِنْسِ وَالْجِنِّ** — "the devils among humans and jinn" (6:112), and His saying: **وَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ فِي أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ** — "and the word became due against them among the nations that had passed away before them of jinn and humans" (41:25), and His saying: **سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ أَيُّهَ الثَّقَلانِ** — "We will soon attend to you, O you two heavy weights!" (55:31) until He says: **يا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَنْ تَنْفُذُوا مِنْ أَقْطارِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ فَانْفُذُوا** — "O company of jinn and humans! If you can pass through the confines of the heavens and the earth, then do pass!" (55:33).

The apparent sense of these verses, by virtue of the contrast (المقابلة) occurring between "man" and "the jinn" (الجان) at times, and between "humans" (الإنس) and "the jinn" (الجن) at others, is that *al-jinn* and *al-jann* are one, even if the expression differs.

The apparent sense of the contrast between His saying: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ** etc., and His saying: **وَالْجَانَّ خَلَقْناهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ** etc., is that the creation of the jinn from "smokeless fire" (نار السموم) refers to the initial creation (الخلق الابتدائي) and the beginning of the appearance of the species, just like the creation of man from dry clay. As for whether the continuation of creation among the individuals of the jinn—which entails the survival of the species—followed the law of the first creation from smokeless fire, unlike man whose creation began from dust and then continued through the sperm-drop (النطفة), His speech is apparently silent on this, except for what is in some of His speech regarding the attribution of progeny (الذرية) to Iblis, as He said: **أَفَتَتَّخِذُونَهُ وَذُرِّيَّتَهُ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِي** — "Will you then take him and his offspring for guardians besides Me?" (18:50), and the attribution of death to them as in His saying: **قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ** (41:25). What is familiar for a species that has progeny and death is procreation (التناسل). The question remains regarding this procreation: is it through mating (سفاد) like some animal species, or through other means?

His saying: **خَلَقْناهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ** — "We created him before"—is detached from its genitive (مقطوع الإضافة), meaning "before the creation of man." The context (القرينة) is the contrast between the two creations.

The fact that the beginning of the jinn's creation is identified in this verse as "smokeless fire" (نار السموم) does not contradict what is in Surah al-Rahman, where it is identified as "a flame of fire" (مارجا من نار)—meaning a flame mixed with smoke—for the two verses summarize that the beginning of its creation was a poisonous wind (ريح سموم) that ignited and became a flame of fire.

Thus, the meaning of the two verses is: "I swear, We began the creation of the human species from clay that dried after it had been liquid, altered, and stinking; and the species of the jinn, We began its creation from a hot, piercing wind that ignited and became fire."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي خالِقٌ بَشَراً** — "When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to create a human...’" (15:28) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "*Al-basharah* (البشرة) is the outer skin, and *al-adamah* (الأدمة) is the inner skin—this is what the general linguists have said... and man is referred to as *bashar* (بشر) in consideration of the visibility of his skin, unlike animals which are covered in wool, hair, or fur. The word *bashar* is used for both singular and plural, and it is dualized as in His saying: **أَنُؤْمِنُ لِبَشَرَيْنِ** (23:47). In the Quran, the term *bashar* is specifically used in every place where the physical body (جثة) and outward appearance of man are considered, such as: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْماءِ بَشَراً** (25:54)." [End of the relevant portion].

His saying: **وَإِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي خالِقٌ بَشَراً** involves an implicit verb, the estimation being: "And remember when your Lord said..." In the speech, there is a transition (التفات) from the first-person plural (تكلم مع الغير) to the third-person (الغيبة). The significance (العناية) in this is similar to the significance previously mentioned in His saying: **وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ** (15:25), for these verses also reveal news that culminates in the Resurrection (الحشر) and eternal felicity or wretchedness.

Furthermore, the first-person plural in the preceding verses—**وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا** (We created) and **خَلَقْناهُ** (We created him)—is of the type where the great speak on behalf of themselves and their servants and assistants out of magnification (تعظيما); that is, by Allah taking His noble angels with Him in the matter. This significance is not appropriate in a context where the angels are being addressed to inform them of His will to create Adam (عليه‌السلام) and His command to them to prostrate to him once He has proportioned him and breathed into him of His spirit. Understand this, and the meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ ساجِدِينَ** — "So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down before him in prostration." (15:29). Proportioning (التسوية) is making a thing balanced and upright in its affair, such that every part of it is as it ought to be. Thus, the proportioning of man is that every one of his organs is in its proper place and in its proper state.

It is not far-fetched to infer from His saying: **إِنِّي خالِقٌ** (I am creating) and **فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ** (when I have proportioned him) that the creation of the first human body occurred through a temporal progression (التدريج الزماني). First was the creation (الخلق), which is the gathering of parts; then the proportioning (التسوية), which is the organizing of the parts and placing each part in its fitting position and state; then the breathing (النفخ). This does not contradict His saying: **خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** — "He created him from dust, then He said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was." (3:59), for His saying: **ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ** etc., refers to the coming into being of the spirit (الروح)—which is the human soul (النفس الإنسانية)—rather than the body, just as He expressed it in another place after describing the gradual creation of the body by saying: **ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْناهُ خَلْقاً آخَرَ** — "then We produced him as another creation" (23:14).

His saying: **وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي** — "and breathed into him of My spirit." Breathing (النفخ) is the introduction of air into the interior of bodies via the mouth or otherwise. It is used metaphorically to denote the casting of an effect or a non-sensory matter into something. In the verse, it means His creation of the human spirit (الروح الإنساني) with its connection and attachment to the body. It is not "inside" it in the way air is inside a blown-into body, as indicated by His saying: **ثُمَّ جَعَلْناهُ نُطْفَةً فِي قَرارٍ مَكِينٍ ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْمُضْغَةَ عِظاماً فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظامَ لَحْماً ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْناهُ خَلْقاً آخَرَ** (23:13-14) and His saying: **قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ** — "Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls...’" (32:11).

The first verse—as you see—explains that the human spirit is the body produced as "another creation," while the body remains as it is without anything being added to it. The second verse explains that the spirit at death is "taken" from the body, while the body remains as it is without anything being subtracted from it.

Thus, the spirit (الروح) is an existent thing in itself that has a type of union (اتحاد) with the body through its attachment to it, and it has independence (استقلال) from the body when its attachment is severed and it departs from it. Some discussion related to this topic has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: **وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتٌ** (2:154) in the first volume of this book.

We hope to fully cover this discussion under His saying: **قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي** — "Say, ‘The spirit is of my Lord’s command’" (17:85), Allah willing.

The attribution of the spirit to Himself in His saying: **مِنْ رُوحِي** (of My spirit) is for honor and nobility (للتكرمة والتشريف), similar to the genitive of possession (الإضافة اللامية). His saying: **فَقَعُوا لَهُ ساجِدِينَ** means "prostrate to him." It is not far-fetched to understand from it "fall down upon the earth prostrating to him," which would signify an emphasis on the angels' submission (الخضوع) to this new creature, as has been said.

The meaning of the verse is: "So when I have balanced his composition, completed the fashioning of his body, and brought into existence the noble spirit attributed to Me, which I link to his body, then fall down and drop to the earth prostrating to him."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَسَجَدَ الْمَلائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ أَبى أَنْ يَكُونَ مَعَ السَّاجِدِينَ** — "Thereat the angels prostrated, all of them together, but not Iblis: he refused to be with those who prostrated." (15:30-31). The word "together" (أجمعون) is an emphasis after an emphasis for intensification. The meaning is that the angels prostrated to him such that not one of them remained [who did not prostrate]. Iblis was excluded from this, and he was not of them, due to His saying: **كانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ** (18:50). As for the claim of those who say that a group of angels were called "jinn" and Iblis was among them, or that "jinn" means "concealment" and thus encompasses angels and others, it is something not to be heeded. We have already presented in the commentary of Surah al-A'raf a discussion on how the command to prostrate included Iblis despite his not being of the angels. The meaning of the two verses is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قالَ يا إِبْلِيسُ ما لَكَ أَلَّا تَكُونَ مَعَ السَّاجِدِينَ** — "He said, ‘O Iblis! What is the matter with you that you are not with those who prostrate?’" (15:32). **ما لَكَ** is a subject and predicate, meaning "What is it that has happened to you?" His saying: **أَلَّا تَكُونَ** is of the type where the preposition is removed (نزع الخافض), the estimation being "in not being with those who prostrate," namely the angels. The sum of the meaning is: "What is wrong with you that you did not prostrate?"

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قالَ لَمْ أَكُنْ لِأَسْجُدَ لِبَشَرٍ خَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ صَلْصالٍ مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ** — "He said, ‘I will not prostrate to a human whom You have created out of a dry clay [drawn] from an aged mud.’" (15:33). In the expression **لَمْ أَكُنْ لِأَسْجُدَ** (I was not one to prostrate), rather than saying "I will not prostrate" (لا أسجد) or "I am not prostrating" (لست أسجد), there is an indication that the refusal to prostrate was a requirement of his very essence (ذاته); it was what was expected of him had his core been known. Thus, the verse conveys through metonymy (الكناية) what His saying in another place conveys explicitly: **أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ** — "I am better than him: You created me from fire and You created him from clay." (38:76).

A discussion has already preceded regarding the meaning of the prostration to Adam, the command to the angels and Iblis to do so, their compliance, and his rebellion, which is useful in this regard in the commentary of Surah al-Baqarah and Surah al-A'raf of this book.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَاخْرُجْ مِنْها فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ إِلى يَوْمِ الدِّينِ** — "He said, ‘Exit from it, for you are indeed an outcast (رجيم), and indeed the curse (اللَّعْنَةَ) shall be upon you until the Day of Retribution.’" (15:34-35). *Al-rajim* (الرجيم) is a form meaning the object of stoning (الرجم), which is expulsion (الطرد); its use became common for expulsion by stones and pebbles. *Al-la'n* (اللعن) is expulsion and distancing from mercy.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ** etc., is in the position of an explanation for His saying: **فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ**, for the expulsion (الرجم) was the cause of his exit from among the angels, from heaven, or from the divine station—in short, from the station of proximity (مقام القرب), which is the level of special divine mercy. Thus, it corresponds to distancing from mercy, which is the curse (اللعن).

Allah, the Glorified, has attributed this curse placed upon Iblis to Himself in another place, saying: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِي إِلى يَوْمِ الدِّينِ** — "and My curse (لَعْنَتِي) shall be upon you until the Day of Retribution" (38:78), and He restricted it in both verses with the phrase: **إِلى يَوْمِ الدِّينِ** (until the Day of Retribution).

As for placing the absolute curse upon him in His saying: **عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ** (the curse be upon you), it is because the curse attaches to sin, and there is no sin except that Iblis has a hand in it through misleading (الإغواء) and whispering (الوسوسة). He is the root to which every sin and its subsequent curse returns, even the curse and ill-consequence that return to the individual sinners themselves. Recall in this regard what was mentioned under His saying: **لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلى بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَجْعَلَهُ فِي جَهَنَّمَ** — "so that Allah may separate the bad from the good, and pile up the bad on one another, and heap them all together, and cast them into hell" (8:37) in the ninth volume of this book.

Furthermore, he—may Allah curse him—is the first to open the door of disobedience to Allah and he disobeyed Him in His command; thus, the ill-consequence of this path returns to him through those who walk it, as long as they walk it.

As for placing His specific curse upon him in His saying: **عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِي** (My curse be upon you), it is because distancing from mercy in reality only takes effect if it is from Him, the Exalted, for no one possesses the power to grant or withhold His mercy except by His permission. Thus, the reality of granting and withholding returns to Him.

Moreover, a curse from other than Allah, the Exalted, is in reality a supplication against him for distancing from mercy. As for the distancing itself, which is the result of the supplication, it is of His act, subsisting through Allah, the Exalted; its reality is the intensification in withholding mercy.

It is said in *al-Majma'*: "Some researchers said: Allah, the Glorified, said here: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ** with the definite article (ال), and said in Surah Sad: **لَعْنَتِي** with the possessive suffix, because there He says: **لِما خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَ** (to what I created with My hands) with the possessive, so He said: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِي** for correspondence. Here He said: **ما لَكَ أَلَّا تَكُونَ مَعَ السَّاجِدِينَ** and carried the verse with the definite article in His saying: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ** and **وَالْجَانَ**, so He also brought the definite article in His saying: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ**." [End of quote]. He also said that in the verse is a clarification that he will never believe.

As for restricting the curse with the phrase **إِلى يَوْمِ الدِّينِ** (until the Day of Retribution), it is because the curse is the title of the sin and ill-consequence returning to the soul from disobedience, and disobedience is limited by the Day of Resurrection; for today is action and no recompense, and tomorrow is recompense and no action. Or if you wish, say: this abode is the abode of recording and preserving deeds, and the Day of Resurrection is the abode of reckoning and recompense.

As for the claim of the one who says that limiting the curse to the Day of Retribution is evidence of it being terminated by it and lifted during it and thereafter, it is something refuted by the apparent sense of the verses clarifying the punishment on the Day of Resurrection.

This is supported by the expression in the verse for the Day of Resurrection as "the Day of Retribution" (يوم الدين), which suggests that he is cursed before the Day of Resurrection and recompensed for it during it. If the punishment were to cease with the arrival of the Hour, the day would be the day of the cessation of retribution, not the Day of Retribution.

It is sometimes said in refuting the problem of the "limit" (الغاية) that it is the furthest limit set by the creatures, so it is like His saying: **خالِدِينَ فِيها ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ** (11:107). This is as you see [weak], and you have already learned the meaning of the verse it is compared to in the commentary of Surah Hud.

It is also said: the "curse" in the verse refers to the curse of the creatures, which ceases with the coming of the Day of Retribution, unlike His curse and His distancing him from His mercy, which continues forever.

It is as if this speaker overlooked His saying in Surah Sad: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِي إِلى يَوْمِ الدِّينِ** (38:78).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قالَ رَبِّ فَأَنْظِرْنِي إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ** — "He said, ‘My Lord! Respite me until the day they are resurrected.’" (15:36). Granting respite (الإنظار) is giving time. He began his speech with **رَبِ** (My Lord) while he was contending with Him, having disobeyed Him and acted arrogantly toward Him, because in the station of supplication (الدعاء), he has no choice but to call upon Him, the Exalted, with that which stirs absolute divine mercy—which is seeking refuge in Him through His Lordship (ربوبيته) over him—so that He might respond to him while he is under wrath.

He began his request with the "fa" of derivation (فاء التفريع) in His saying: **فَأَنْظِرْنِي** (So respite me), and he mentioned the resurrection of all mankind without specifically mentioning Adam, their father, who was the cause of his expulsion and curse for refusing to prostrate to him. All of this is based on what preceded in the commentary of the story's verses in Surah al-A'raf: that the command was to prostrate to all of mankind, and Adam (عليه‌السلام) was like the focal point (القنبلة) set for prostration, representing the human species.

To clarify: it has already preceded regarding His saying: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْناكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ** — "Certainly We created you, then We formed you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam.’ So they [all] prostrated, but not Iblis..." (7:11), that they were commanded to prostrate to the human species, not to the person of Adam (عليه‌السلام) alone. This prostration was not a social honor without a real purpose; rather, it was a submission (خضوع) according to creation (الخلقة). Thus, according to what was intended by their creation, they are submissive to man according to what was intended by the perfection of his creation; that is, they are subjected (مسخرون) for his sake, working toward the felicity of his life. This means that man has a station of proximity and a stage of perfected felicity that exceeds that of the angels.

Their collective prostration to him is evidence that they are all subjected in the path of his perfection in felicity, working for his success and prosperity—such as the angels of life, the angels of death, the angels of sustenance, the angels of revelation, the pursuers (المعقبات), the guardians (الحفظة), the scribes (الكتبة), and others mentioned in scattered Quranic verses. Thus, the angels are divine means (أسباب إلهية) and assistants to man in the path of his felicity and perfection.

From here, it becomes clear to the astute contemplator that Iblis's refusal to prostrate was a refusal on his part to submit to the human species and to work toward its felicity and assist it in its required perfection, contrary to what was shown by the angels. Thus, by his refusal to prostrate, he exited from the assembly of the angels, as indicated by His saying: **ما لَكَ أَلَّا تَكُونَ مَعَ السَّاجِدِينَ**, and he manifested hostility toward the human species and disavowal of them as long as they live and exist, or eternally.

This is supported by Allah's placing the absolute curse upon him from the day he refused until the Day of Retribution—which is the duration of the human species' stay in this world. He placed it upon him as such even before Iblis claimed that he would mislead them and before he said: **لأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** (I will surely mislead them all). This suggests that his refusal to prostrate was a type of hostility and enmity from him toward this species, from Adam to the last of his progeny who will be born and live.

It is as if he—may he be cursed—understood from His saying: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ إِلى يَوْمِ الدِّينِ** that he has a role with the human species until the Day of Resurrection, and that their wretchedness and the corruption of their deeds are linked to him by virtue of his refusal to prostrate. Therefore, he asked for respite until the day they are resurrected, deriving that from the curse placed upon him, saying: **رَبِّ فَأَنْظِرْنِي إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ**. He did not say: "My Lord, respite me until the day Adam dies," or "respite me as long as he lives," but rather he mentioned Adam and all his children and requested respite until the day they are resurrected, deriving this from the curse until the Day of Retribution. When he was granted what he asked, he revealed what was hidden in his essence and said: **لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ**.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنْظَرِينَ إِلى يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ** — "He said, ‘You are indeed among the respited, until the day of the known time (يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ).’" (15:37-38). This is a response from Allah, the Glorified, to Iblis, containing both an acceptance and a rejection. As for the acceptance, it is regarding the principle of respite that he requested. As for the rejection, it is regarding the condition—that the respite be until the day they are resurrected. For it is clear and apparent from the context of the two verses that the "day of the known time" is other than the "day they are resurrected." Thus, He did not allow him respite until the day they are resurrected, but until a day other than it, which is inevitably before the Day of Resurrection.

By this, the error of the one who says that he—may he be cursed—was granted exactly what he asked for, and that the two days in the verses are one, becomes clear. One of their arguments is His saying in Surah al-A'raf regarding the story: **قالَ إِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنْظَرِينَ** (7:15) without restricting it to anything.

As for the falsity of their claim that the two days in the verses are identical, it has appeared from what preceded. As for the falsity of arguing from the absolute nature of the verse in al-A'raf, it is because it is restricted by the restriction in this Surah and Surah Sad with the phrase: **إِلى يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ**. This is common and widespread in His speech, and the Quran bears witness to itself and speaks through itself.

The apparent meaning of "the day of the known time" is a time specified in the divine knowledge, similar to His saying: **وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ** — "and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21) and His saying: **أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ رِزْقٌ مَعْلُومٌ** — "For them there is a known provision" (37:41). Thus, it is certainly known to Allah. As for whether it is known to Iblis or unknown to him, it is not known from the wording. The statement of some that Allah kept the day vague and did not clarify it, so it is known to Allah but unknown to Iblis because clarifying it would be an incitement to disobedience, is a statement lacking evidence. The vagueness of the wording for us is not the same as the vagueness of the speech addressed to Iblis for him. Furthermore, the "incitement of Iblis to disobedience"—when he is the root of every assumed disobedience—is not without difficulty; so understand this.

Moreover, Iblis's subsequent statement: **لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** is evidence that he will remain until the last of mankind live on earth whom he can mislead. He had understood from His saying: **إِلى يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ** that it is the end of the lifespan of the humans living on earth whom he is permitted to mislead.

It is attributed to Ibn Abbas and favored by the majority that the day is the last of the days of legal responsibility (التكليف)—which is the first blast (النفخة الأولى) on the day the creatures die. It is as if this is based on the idea that Iblis remains as long as legal responsibility remains and opposition and disobedience are possible, which is the duration of man's life in the world. This ends with the first blast by which the creatures die; that is the "day of the known time" until which Allah respited him. Between it and the second blast—in which they are resurrected—are four hundred years or forty years according to different narrations; this is the difference between what Iblis asked for and what Allah, the Glorified, granted.

This would be a good explanation were it not for their statement: "Iblis remains as long as legal responsibility remains and opposition and disobedience are possible," for this is a premise that is neither self-evident nor demonstrated. The people's reliance in this is that what is inferred from the verses and reports is that every disbelief and transgression existing in the human species is attributed to Iblis's misleading and whispering, as indicated by verses such as His saying: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ** — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy’?" (36:60) and His saying: **وَقالَ الشَّيْطانُ لَمَّا قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ إِنَّ اللهَ وَعَدَكُمْ وَعْدَ الْحَقِّ وَوَعَدْتُكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُكُمْ** etc. (14:22). Their implication is that Iblis's existence must continue as long as legal responsibility remains, and legal responsibility remains as long as man remains; this is the desired conclusion.

The flaw in this is that while the fact that human disobedience is generally attributed to Iblis's misleading is inferred from the verses and narrations without doubt, it only necessitates Iblis's survival as long as disobedience and perversion (الغواية) remain, not his survival as long as legal responsibility remains. There is no evidence for the necessary connection between disobedience and legal responsibility in terms of existence.

Rather, the proof is established from both reason and tradition that the species-goal of man—which is felicity (السعادة)—will eventually encompass the species, and the human society will be liberated toward goodness and righteousness. On that day, none but Allah, the Glorified, will be worshipped on earth. At that time, the carpet of disbelief and transgression will be folded, life will become pure, and the diseases of the hearts and the whisperings of the breasts will be removed. We have already detailed this in the discussions on Prophethood in the second volume and in the stories of Noah in the tenth volume of this book. Allah says: **ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أِيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ** — "Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what the hands of the people have wrought, so that He may let them taste some of [the retribution for] what they have done, so that they may turn back." (30:41) and He said: **وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ** — "Certainly We wrote in the Psalms, after the Torah: ‘Indeed My righteous servants shall inherit the earth.’" (21:105).

From this, it appears that the proof they relied upon only indicates that the "day of the known time" which Allah made the limit of Iblis's respite is the day Allah, the Glorified, reforms human society, such that the root of corruption is cut off and none but Allah is worshipped on that day—not the day the creatures die by the first blast.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قالَ رَبِّ بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** — "He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversion (أَغْوَيْتَنِي), I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them all, except Your exclusive servants (المخلصين) among them.’" (15:39-40). The "ba" in His saying: **بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي** is for causality (السببية) and the "ma" is infinitive (مصدرية), meaning: "I take Your consigning me to perversion as a cause to glamorize for them, and I cast upon them what has settled in me of perversion," just as they will say on the Day of Resurrection as Allah narrated: **أَغْوَيْناهُمْ كَما غَوَيْنا** — "We perverted them as we were perverted ourselves" (28:63).

The statement of some that the "ba" is for an oath (للقسم)—meaning "I swear by Your consigning me to perversion, I will surely glamorize"—is among the poorest of statements. It has never been known in the Book or the Sunnah to swear by something like perversion and misguidance, nor is there anything understood from it of the magnification (التعظيما) necessary in an oath.

He—may he be cursed—attributed in his saying **بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي** to Allah, the Glorified, that He perverted him, and Allah did not reject it nor respond to it. His intention by this was not his initial perversion when he disobeyed the command to prostrate and did not prostrate to Adam (عليه‌السلام). The evidence for this is that there is no link between his own disobedience and the disobedience of man to his Lord such that his disobedience would be the cause of their disobedience and he would use it to pervert them.

Rather, he means by it what is conveyed by His saying: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ إِلى يَوْمِ الدِّينِ** regarding the settling of the absolute curse in him, which is distancing from mercy and misguiding from the path of felicity. This is a perversion (إغواء) that is the effect of the perversion (الغواية) he manifested from himself and brought from his own side; thus, it is of His misguiding as a recompense (مجازاة), not an initial misguiding, which is permissible and not impossible for Allah, the Exalted. Therefore, He did not reject it, as He said: **يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** — "He misleads many by it and guides many by it; and He misleads no one by it except the transgressors" (2:26). We have explained this under that verse and in other places in this book.

At this point, the meaning of causality becomes sound—namely, his perverting people because of the perversion that touched him and settled in him. For when distancing from mercy and remoteness from felicity became inseparable from his soul by the inseparability of the divine curse from him, whenever he approached the heart of a human through whispering and glamorizing, or took control of a soul while he himself was distant from mercy and felicity, that necessitated the distancing of the one he approached or dominated. This is his perverting them by casting the effect of the perversion he possesses onto them, which is evident.

This is what contemplation of the verse yields. Its summary is that the intended "perversion" (الإغواء) is not initial misguidance, but misguidance by way of recompense, which is indicated by His saying: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِي** etc.

As for the [other] commentators, it was as if they took it as a given that His saying: **بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي**, if taken in its apparent meaning of misguidance, would be initial misguidance and would refer to his refusal and withholding from prostration. Therefore, they found the verse problematic and differed in the interpretation of "perversion" according to their differing schools of thought regarding the attribution of evil to Allah, the Exalted, and its issuance from Him in terms of permissibility and impossibility.

Some of them—the proponents of predestination (أهل الجبر)—said: the attribution of perversion to Him, the Exalted, without denial from Him, indicates that evil, like good, is from Allah. The meaning is: "My Lord, because You misled me through the refusal to prostrate—so it is from You—I swear I will surely mislead them all."

Others—the non-predestinarians—said: it is not permissible to attribute evil, disobedience, and every ugly thing to Him, the Exalted. They interpreted the verse in several ways:
1. That "perversion" (الإغواء) in the verse means "disappointment" (التخييب). The meaning is: "My Lord, because You have disappointed me of Your mercy, I will surely disappoint them by calling them to Your disobedience."
2. That the intended "perversion" is misguidance from the path of Paradise. The meaning is: "Because You misled me from the path of Your Paradise due to the disobedience that issued from me, I will surely mislead them by the call [to evil]."
3. That the meaning of His saying: **بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي** is: "Because You burdened me with a command at which I went astray through disobedience"—namely, the prostration. He called that "misguiding" from Him metaphorically (توسعا). By reflecting on what we have presented, you will know that the verse is in no need of this discussion and the interpretations offered therein.

Similar to this discussion is their discussion regarding the respite occurring in His saying: **فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنْظَرِينَ**, from the perspective that it leads to ugly perversion and is a preference of the less-preferred (المرجوح) over the preferred (الراجح).

Those who permit it said: the verse indicates that the "goodness" and "ugliness" by which reason explains our actions have no influence on His actions, the Exalted. He may reward whom He wills and punish whom He wills without a justifying cause, even with the preference of the opposite. They said: "Whoever claims that a wise person would confine a group in a house and release into it a great fire and many lethal vipers, yet did not intend to harm any of those people by burning or stinging, has exited from the human primordial nature (الفطرة الإنسانية). Therefore, by the judgment of the primordial nature, Allah, the Exalted, intended by respiting Iblis to mislead some people."

Those who forbid it interpret the respite as being because He, the Exalted, knew of Iblis and his followers that they would die in disbelief and transgression and go to the Fire whether He respited Iblis or not. Furthermore, He, the Exalted, compensated for His support of that with the increased reward of the God-conscious believers. Moreover, he says: **وَلأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ** (I will surely pervert them); if the perversion were from Allah, he would have denied it to Him—among other interpretations they brought.

I wonder what made them overlook the verses of trial (الامتحان) and tribulation (الابتلاء) despite their abundance, such as His saying: **لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ** — "so that Allah may separate the bad from the good" (8:37) and His saying: **وَلِيَبْتَلِيَ اللهُ ما فِي صُدُورِكُمْ وَلِيُمَحِّصَ ما فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ** — "and so that Allah may test what is in your breasts, and that He may purge what is in your hearts" (3:154), and other verses indicating that the system of felicity and wretchedness, reward and punishment, is built on the foundation of trial and tribulation. Man is situated between good and evil, felicity and wretchedness; he has what he chooses of action with its results.

If there were not a caller to good—namely the noble angels, or if you wish, say: it is Allah—and a caller to evil—namely Iblis and his tribe—trial would have no meaning. Allah says: **الشَّيْطانُ يَعِدُكُمُ الْفَقْرَ وَيأْمُرُكُمْ بِالْفَحْشاءِ وَاللهُ يَعِدُكُمْ مَغْفِرَةً مِنْهُ وَفَضْلاً** — "Satan frightens you of poverty and prompts you to indecency, and Allah promises you His forgiveness and grace." (2:268).

If Allah supported Iblis against man by respiting him until the day of the known time, He also supported man against him by the angels who remain as long as the world remains. Allah, the Glorified, did not say to him: "You are respited," but rather said: **فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنْظَرِينَ** (You are among the respited), thus affirming others who are respited and making him one of them.

If He supported him with the ability to glamorize falsehood—of disbelief and transgression—for man, He supported man by guiding him to the truth, glamorizing faith in his heart, fashioning him upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) of monotheism (التوحيد), making known to him lewdness and godwariness (التقوى), and giving him a light by which he walks among the people if he believes in his Lord—among other favors. He said: **قُلِ اللهُ يَهْدِي لِلْحَقِّ** — "Say, ‘Allah guides to the truth.’" (10:35) and He said: **وَلكِنَّ اللهَ حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْإيمانَ وَزَيَّنَهُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ** — "but Allah has endeared the faith to you and glamorized it in your hearts" (49:7) and He said: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها** — "So set your heart as a person of pure faith on the religion (الدين) — *the primordial nature (الفطرة) of Allah according to which He originated mankind*" (30:30) and He said: **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها** — "by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [discernment of] its vices and its godwariness" (91:7-8) and He said: **أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ** — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people..." (6:122) and He said: **إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ** — "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith in the life of the world and on the day when the witnesses rise up" (40:51). The use of the first-person plural (تكلم بالغير) suggests the mediation of the angels.

Thus, man was created in himself unarmed, possessing nothing of felicity or wretchedness according to the beginning of his creation, standing at the crossroads of two paths: the path of good and obedience—which is the path of the angels, who have nothing but obedience—and the path of evil and disobedience—which is the path of Iblis and his soldiers, who have nothing but opposition and disobedience. Whichever of the two paths he inclines toward in the course of his life, he falls into it, and its companions accompany him, glamorizing for him what they possess and guiding him to where their path ends—which is Paradise or the Fire, and felicity or wretchedness.

It has become clear from what preceded that respiting Iblis until the day of the known time is not a preference of the less-preferred over the preferred, nor a nullification of the law of causality (قانون العلية); rather, it is so that through him and his opposite—the survival of the angels—that which is necessary of the matter of trial and tribulation may be facilitated. Thus, there is no place for the problem.

His saying: **لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth"—means "I will surely glamorize falsehood for them" or "I will surely glamorize sins for them," as has been said. The first meaning is more comprehensive. The object is omitted in any case. It appears that the object is disregarded and the verb is used intransitively; the purpose is to state the principle of glamorizing (التزيين) as a metonymy for deception (الغرور). It is said: "he glamorized such and such for him," meaning he incited him to it through deception. The pronoun in **لَهُمْ** (for them) refers to Adam and his progeny as indicated by the context. The meaning of "glamorizing for them on the earth" is their deception in this earthly life, which is the life of the world (الحياة الدنيا); it is the proximate cause for perversion. Thus, the conjunction of His saying: **وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** to it is the conjunction of the effect to the cause that results in it.

The verse suggests, rather indicates, what we presented in the commentary on the verses of Adam's Paradise in the first volume of this book: that Adam's disobedience in eating from the forbidden tree due to Iblis's whispering was not a disobedience to a legislative command (أمر مولوي), but rather a violation of an advisory command (أمر إرشادي) that does not entail a breach in his infallibility (عصمته). For He identifies the earth in the verse as the theater for his glamorizing and perverting. His deception of Adam and his spouse in Paradise was only to expel them from it and bring them down to the earth so they might procreate therein, and he might pervert them and their children from the truth and mislead them from the path. Allah says: **يا بَنِي آدَمَ لا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ كَما أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُمْ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ يَنْزِعُ عَنْهما لِباسَهُما لِيُرِيَهُما سَوْآتِهِما** — "O children of Adam! Do not let Satan tempt you, like he expelled your parents from paradise, stripping them of their garments to expose to them their nakedness." (7:27).

His saying: **إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** — "except Your exclusive servants (المخلصين) among them." He excluded from the generality of perversion a specific group of humans, namely the "exclusive ones" (المخلصين)—with the *fathah* on the *lam* according to the famous recitation. The context bears witness that they are those who were sincere (أخلصوا) to Allah, and none made them exclusive (أخلصهم) except Allah, the Glorified. We have already presented in the discussion on sincerity (الإخلاص) in the commentary of Surah Yusuf that the "exclusive ones" (المخلصين) are those whom Allah has made exclusive to Himself after they made themselves sincere to Allah. Thus, none other than Him, the Glorified, has a share in them, nor is there a place in their hearts [for others]. Whatever Satan casts to them of his snares and glamorizations turns into a remembrance of Allah that draws them closer to Him.

From here, it is preferred that the exclusion is from "perversion" (الإغواء) only, not from it and "glamorizing" (التزيين); meaning that he—may he be cursed—glamorizes for everyone, but he only perverts those other than the exclusive ones.

It is inferred from the exclusion of "the servants" (العباد) first, then its interpretation as "the exclusive ones" (المخلصين), that the right of servitude (العبودية) is only fulfilled by Allah making the servant exclusive to Himself; that is, that none possesses him but He. This returns to the idea that the human does not see any ownership (ملكا) for himself, and that he does not own himself nor any of his attributes, effects, or actions, and that ownership (الملك)—with both the *kasrah* and *dammah* on the *mim*—belongs to Allah alone.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قالَ هذا صِراطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ** — "He said, ‘This is a straight path [leading] to Me.’" (15:41). The apparent sense of the speech, as yielded by the context, is that it is a metonymy for the fact that the matter belongs to Him, the Exalted; there is no independence from Him in it in any way. Just as the fact that the ship's path is upon the sea dictates to its passengers that they have no escape from what the crossing of the water requires of equipment and means, and likewise the fact that the caravan's path is upon the mountain necessitates what is required to cross its lofty peaks and difficult passes—so too, its being a path "upon Him" (عَلَيَّ) with "straightness" (مُسْتَقِيمٌ) means that it is a matter dependent in every aspect upon His judgment and decree. For He is Allah, from Whom everything begins and to Whom everything ends; no matter is realized except that He is its Lord, the Self-Subsisting (القيوم) over it.

The apparent context also indicates that the reference in His saying: **هذا صِراطٌ** etc., is to Iblis's statement: **لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ**. When he manifested by this statement that he would take revenge upon them and spread his authority over them all through glamorizing and perverting, such that none would be saved from them except a few, it was as if he was suggesting that he would be independent in what he resolved upon and would prevail with his will over Allah, the Glorified, regarding what He intended by their creation, their vicegerency, and their servitude—as Allah narrated from his statement in another place: **وَلا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شاكِرِينَ** — "and You will not find most of them to be grateful" (7:17).

Thus, the meaning of the verse is: "What you mentioned—that you will pervert them all and your exclusion of those you excluded, and your manifesting its attribution to your power and will, claiming that you are independent in it—is a matter that none possesses but Me, none judges in it but Me, and it does not issue except from My decree. If you pervert, it is by My permission you pervert; and if I prevent, it is by My will I prevent. Nothing of the matter belongs to you, nor of ownership except what I have made you own, nor of power except what I have empowered you with. And the authority (السلطان) I decree for you is that 'My servants—you have no authority over them, except such of the perverted as follow you' etc."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ** — "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverted (الْغاوِينَ) as follow you." (15:42). This is the decree (القضاء) that He, the Glorified, referred to in the previous verse regarding the matter of perversion, mentioning that it belongs to Him alone; no one else has any hand or share in it.

Its summary is that Adam and all his children are His servants, not as Iblis said when he restricted His servants to the exclusive ones among them, saying: **إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ**. Allah, the Glorified, did not give him authority over them—that is, over the servants—such that he could be independent in their affair and pervert them. Rather, He only gave him authority over a group of them, namely those who followed him from among the perverted (الْغاوِينَ), who took him as the guardian of their affair and handed over to him the reins of their management. These are the ones over whom he has authority.

If you examine the verse closely, you will find it refuting Iblis's statement: **لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** from three primary aspects:
First: He restricted His servants to the exclusive ones among them and denied his own authority over them, while generalizing his authority over the rest. Allah, the Glorified, generalized His servants to include everyone and restricted Iblis's authority to a group of them—those who followed him from among the perverted—and denied his authority over the rest.

Second: He—may he be cursed—claimed for himself independence (الاستقلال) in perverting them, as appears from his saying **لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ** in the context of contention and the rebuke of revenge. Allah, the Glorified, refutes him, stating that it is a false claim on his part; rather, it is by a decree from Allah and an authority through His empowering him. He only made him possess the perversion of those who followed him and were perverted in themselves by their own bad choice.

Thus, Iblis did not bring anything from himself, nor did he spoil any matter for his Lord—neither in his perverting the people of perversion, for it is by a decree from Allah, the Glorified, that their perversion settles upon them because of him—and he—may he be cursed—partially admitted this by saying: **رَبِّ بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي**—nor in his exclusion of the exclusive ones, for that too is by a pervasive decree from Allah. Thus, there is no judgment but Allah's.

This which the noble verse conveys—namely, the empowering of Iblis to pervert the perverted who are perverted in themselves, and the liberation of the exclusive ones who are exclusive in themselves from his plot, all of that by a decree from Allah—is built upon a great principle yielded by the Quranic monotheism (التوحيد القرآني), conveyed by verses such as His saying: **إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ** — "Sovereignty belongs only to Allah" (12:67) and His saying: **وَهُوَ اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْأُولى وَالْآخِرَةِ وَلَهُ الْحُكْمُ** — "He is Allah, there is no god except Him. All praise belongs to Him in the first and the last, and to Him belongs the judgment" (28:70) and His saying: **الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ** — "The truth is from your Lord" (3:60) and His saying: **وَيُحِقُّ اللهُ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ** — "and Allah will confirm the truth by His words" (10:82), and other verses indicating that every judgment, positive or negative, is owned by Allah and pervasive by His decree.

From here, the laxity in their interpretation of His saying: **إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ** becomes apparent. They said: "If he accepts from Iblis and follows him, Iblis gains authority over him by his turning away from guidance to the perversion he calls him to." Its apparent sense is that it is a coercive authority (سلطان قهري) that Iblis obtains through their bad choice, not from himself nor by a placement from Allah, the Glorified.

The aspect of its flaw: it involves taking independence and innate power (الحول الذاتي) from Iblis and giving it to the essences of things. If Iblis does not own anything from himself and without the permission of his Lord, then things and matters also do not own for themselves anything or any judgment—even the necessities and the requirements of essences—except by permission from Allah and His making them own it; so understand this.

Third: His authority to pervert those he perverts, even if it is by a placement and empowering from Allah, the Glorified, is not an empowering for initial perversion and misguidance—which is not permissible to attribute to His presence, the Glorified. Rather, it is an empowering for perversion by way of recompense (المجازاة), preceded by their own perversion from their own side and in themselves.

The evidence for this is His saying: **إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ**. Iblis only perverts the one who follows him through his [own] perversion; that is, the human follows him through his perversion first, then he [Iblis] perverts him second. Thus, there is a perversion (غواية) followed by a consigning to perversion (إغواء). The perversion is a crime from the human, and the consigning to perversion because of Iblis is a recompense from Allah, the Glorified.

If this misleading (الإغواء) were a primary misleading by Iblis of someone who did not deserve it, then he would be more worthy of blame than the human being, as he mentions on the Day of Resurrection according to what the Glorified narrates of his saying: **وَمَا كَانَ لِيَ عَلَيْكُم مِّن سُلْطَانٍ إِلَّا أَن دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَاسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِي ۖ فَلَا تَلُومُونِي وَلُومُوا أَنفُسَكُم** — "I had no authority over you, except that I called you and you responded to me. So do not blame me, but blame yourselves" (Ibrahim: 22). Thus, the blame lies with the criminal human being, and he is responsible for his disobedience rather than Iblis.

Indeed, Iblis is blamed for the action he is clothed in by his own evil choice, which is the misleading (الإغواء) that Allah gave him power over as a recompense for his refusal to prostrate to Adam when he was commanded. Thus, misleading is that over which his authority (ولايته) became established, as the Glorified points out in another place in His speech where He says: **إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا الشَّيَاطِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — "Indeed We have made the devils friends of those who do not have faith" (Al-A'raf: 27). And the Exalted said—and this is the clearest support for all we have presented: **كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ مَن تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُ يُضِلُّهُ وَيَهْدِيهِ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ السَّعِيرِ** — "It has been ordained for him that whoever takes him for a friend, he will mislead him and guide him to the punishment of the Blaze" (Al-Hajj: 4).

It has been gathered from what preceded that the meaning of His saying: "My servants" (عِبَادِي) refers to human beings in general; that the exception in His saying: "except such as follow you" (مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ) is connected (متصل) and not severed (منقطع); that the word "who" (مَنِ) in His saying: "of the perverse" (مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ) is explanatory (بيانية); and that the discourse is built upon refuting the claim of Iblis. Furthermore, the verse contains two decrees from Allah, the Glorified, regarding the status of the excepted and the non-excepted, among other points.

From this, the lack of soundness in the view of some becomes apparent, who claim that "My servants" refers only to those whom Iblis excepted and expressed by his saying: "Your servants among them" (عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ). According to them, the exception would be severed (منقطع), and the speech would be intended to confirm Iblis's claim that he has authority over those he misleads, and that the purified ones (المخلصين) are beyond his reach. The meaning would then be: "The purified ones—you have no authority over them, but you are given power over those who follow you of the perverse."

You know, by reflecting on what preceded, that this destroys the foundation of the context (السياق) and what the station of disputation (المخاصمة) provides. It would be unworthy of the Presence of Might and Grandeur (ساحة العزة والكبرياء) to reduce the speech of the Exalted to a level where it provides nothing more than a change in the form of Iblis's words while preserving his meaning as a confirmation or admission. For he says: "I will mislead them except the purified," and Allah, the Glorified, says: "You do not mislead the purified, but you mislead others!"

Some have interpreted His saying: "My servants" (عِبَادِي) as all of humanity, while still taking the exception as severed (منقطع). Perhaps this was based on the impermissibility of excepting the majority of individuals—for one does not say, "He owes me a hundred except ninety-nine," for example—and it is well known that the perverse (الغاوين) among people are incomparably more numerous than the purified (المخلصين).

The response to this is that such a rule only applies when the exception looks at the explicit number. However, if what is looked at is the type (النوع) or the category (الصنف) by its title, there is no harm in the number of individuals being greater. Human beings have several categories: the purified (المخلصون), the believers below them, the oppressed (المستضعفون), and the perverse (الغاوون) who followed Iblis. The last category was excepted in the verse by its title, while the others remained, and they are multiple categories.

Some made the exception severed (منقطع) to avoid affirming the authority (سلطان) of Iblis even over the perverse, imagining that it contradicts the absolute nature of Divine Sovereignty or His Justice. The meaning of the verse according to this view is: "My servants—you have no authority over them, but those who followed you of the perverse have handed over the reins of their souls to you and granted you authority over themselves; this is not from yourself such that you could frustrate Allah in His creation, nor from Allah such that it would contradict His Justice."

The response is: He has authority over the perverse, not from himself, but by a placement (جعل) from Allah. This does not contradict His Justice in His creation, for it is an authority of recompense (تسليط مجازاة), not a primary authority (تسليط ابتدائي). There is no contradiction between the authority being by a decree (قضاء) from the Exalted and its being due to the perverse following him by their own choice. All of this has been clarified in what we presented.

Furthermore, His saying regarding him: **كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ مَن تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُ يُضِلُّهُ** — "It has been ordained for him that whoever takes him for a friend, he will mislead him" (Al-Hajj: 4) and His saying: **إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا الشَّيَاطِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — "Indeed We have made the devils friends of those who do not have faith" (Al-A'raf: 27) explicitly indicate the establishment of his authority and that it is by the placement and decree of Allah, the Glorified.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** — **"Indeed hell is the tryst of them all"** (Al-Hijr: 43). It appears that "tryst" (موعد) is a noun of place (اسم مكان), and the meaning of Hell being their tryst is that it is the place for the fulfillment of the punishment Allah promised them.

This is a confirmation from the Glorified of the establishment of His power and that the entire matter returns to Him. It is as if the Exalted is saying to him: "The authority you mentioned over the perverse is not from yourself, nor have you frustrated Us; rather, We gave you power over them because of their following you, and furthermore, We shall soon recompense them with the punishment of Hell."

Because the speech is intended to explain their condition, He restricted it to mentioning their recompense and did not mention Iblis or his recompense with them, unlike His saying: **لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكَ وَمِمَّن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** — "I will surely fill hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together" (Sad: 85) and His saying: **فَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ جَزَاؤُكُمْ جَزَاءً مَّوْفُورًا** — "then hell shall be your recompense, an ample recompense" (Al-Isra: 63), because the context here is different.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **لَهَا سَبْعَةُ أَبْوَابٍ لِّكُلِّ بَابٍ مِّنْهُمْ جُزْءٌ مَّقْسُومٌ** — **"It has seven gates, and to each gate belongs a separate portion of them"** (Al-Hijr: 44). The Glorified has not clarified in any of His explicit speech what is meant by these "gates" (أبواب). Are they like the gates of walls—entrances that lead everyone to a single courtyard? Or are they levels (طبقات) and degrees (دركات) that differ in the type and intensity of punishment?

Often, in matters of various types, each type is called a "gate" (باب), as it is said: the gates of good, the gates of evil, and the gates of mercy. The Exalted said: **فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ أَبْوَابَ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "We opened for them the gates of all things" (Al-An'am: 44). Sometimes the causes (أسباب) of a thing and the paths to reaching it are called gates, such as the "gates of sustenance" for various types of earnings and transactions.

It is not far-fetched that the second meaning is derived from various verses regarding the Fire, such as His saying: **وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ زُمَرًا ۖ حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءُوهَا فُتِحَتْ أَبْوَابُهَا** — "The faithless will be driven to hell in throngs. When they reach it, its gates will be opened..." until He says: **قِيلَ ادْخُلُوا أَبْوَابَ جَهَنَّمَ خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا** — "It will be said, ‘Enter the gates of hell to remain in it [forever]’" (Al-Zumar: 71-72), and His saying: **إِنَّ الْمُنَافِقِينَ فِي الدَّرْكِ الْأَسْفَلِ مِنَ النَّارِ** — "Indeed the hypocrites will be in the lowest reach of the Fire" (Al-Nisa: 145), among other verses.

This is supported by His saying: "to each gate belongs a separate portion of them," for its apparent meaning is that the "portion" (الجزء) itself is divided and distributed among the gates. This only suits "gate" in the sense of a level (طبقة), rather than "gate" in the sense of an entrance. As for the interpretation by some that the "separate portion" refers to a specific group distinguished from others, its weakness is apparent.

Accordingly, Hell having seven gates means that the punishment prepared therein is diversified into seven types, and then each type is divided into sections according to the division of the portion that enters and remains in it. This necessitates the division of the sins that cause entry into it into seven categories, as well as the division of the paths leading to and the causes inviting those sins into that same division. By this, the narrations (الروايات) mentioned regarding these meanings are supported, as will come to you, if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ ادْخُلُوها بِسَلامٍ آمِنِينَ** — **"Indeed the God-conscious will be amid gardens and springs. ‘Enter them in peace and safety!’"** (Al-Hijr: 45-46). That is, they are established in gardens and springs, and it is said to them: "Enter them with a peace (سلام) that cannot be described or fully grasped, while you are secure (آمنين) from every evil and harm."

When the Glorified mentioned His decree regarding those who follow Iblis of the perverse, He mentioned what He decreed for the God-conscious (المتقين) regarding Paradise. The interpretation of godwariness (التقوى) — *the protective shield of piety and restraint from sin* — has been mentioned in the speech of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as refraining from the prohibitions of Allah. Since their glad tidings of Paradise are repeated in the speech of the Exalted, the God-conscious are more general than the purified (المخلصين).

As for what has been said—that there is no doubt the context indicates the God-conscious are the purified ones mentioned previously, and that the absolute (المطلق) is carried upon the perfect individual (الفرد الكامل)—

The response is that this is based on the assumption that "My servants" in His saying: "Indeed, as for My servants, you shall have no authority over them" refers only to the purified, such that the context is restricted to them. However, it has preceded that "My servants" refers to the general members of humanity; the perverse were excluded by the exception, and the rest remained. The Glorified mentioned His decree for the perverse as being the Fire, and here He mentions His decree for others among those for whom He has made Paradise mandatory. The matter of the oppressed (المستضعفون) is deferred, and the matter of the sinners among the people of major sins who die without repentance is linked to intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah*. Thus, the people of godwariness among the believers remain, and they are more general than the purified; so Paradise was decreed for them.

As for the notion of carrying the absolute (المطلق) upon the perfect individual (الفرد الكامل), it is an error; rather, it is carried upon the common individual (الفرد المتعارف), and the detail of this matter is in the science of principles (فن الأصول).

Imam al-Razi mentioned in his *Tafsir* that the "God-conscious" in the verse refers to those who avoided polytheism (الشرك), attributing this to the majority of the Companions and Successors and basing it on a report.

He said: "This is the correct truth. What indicates this is that the 'God-conscious' (المتقي) is one who performs an act of godwariness (التقوى) even once, just as the 'striker' (الضارب) is one who performs the act of striking once. It is not a condition for the truth of the description of being God-conscious that one performs all types of godwariness. What establishes this is that one who performs a single instance of godwariness is performing godwariness, for the individual instance necessarily contains the essence (الماهية). Every performer of godwariness must be God-conscious; therefore, the performer of a single instance must be God-conscious. For this reason, they said: the apparent command does not imply repetition. Thus, the literal sense of the verse necessitates the attainment of gardens and springs for everyone who has guarded against even a single sin, except that the Ummah is in consensus that guarding against disbelief (الكفر) is a condition for the attainment of this ruling.

Furthermore, this verse came after the saying of Iblis: 'except Your servants among them, the purified,' and after the saying of the Exalted: 'Indeed, as for My servants, you shall have no authority over them.' Therefore, faith (الإيمان) was considered in this ruling, and it is mandatory that no other restriction be added. Since the specification (تخصيص) of a general term (العام) is contrary to the literal sense (الظاهر), the less the specification, the more it accords with the original principle (الأصل) and the literal sense.

Thus, it is established that the mentioned ruling covers all those who say: 'There is no god but Allah, Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah,' even if they are people of disobedience. This is a clear demonstration and obvious speech." End quote.

The implication of his speech is the inclusion in the verse of anyone who avoided polytheism, even if he committed all the destructive major sins (الكبائر الموبقة) for which the Mighty Book explicitly states the deserving of the Fire, and abandoned all the obligations for which it explicitly states the same for their abandonment. One who is familiar with His speech, the Exalted, and reflects upon His verses, will not doubt that the Quran does not call such a person "God-conscious" (متقياً), nor does it count him among the God-conscious. The Quran has frequently mentioned the God-conscious and given them explicit glad tidings of Paradise in nearly twenty places, describing them in many of those places as avoiding prohibitions; and by this, godwariness (التقوى) was interpreted in the hadith, as preceded.

Furthermore, the mere correctness of applying a description is one thing, and naming (التسمية) is another. One is not named among the believers (المؤمنين), the doers of good (المحسنين), the obedient (القانتين), the purified (المخلصين), or the patient (الصابرين)—especially in descriptions that imply permanence and continuity—except one in whom the description has become established. If what he mentioned regarding the God-conscious were correct, the same would apply to the wrongdoers (الظالمين), the transgressors (الفاسقين), the corrupters (المفسدين), the criminals (المجرمين), the perverse (الغاوين), and the astray (الضالين), whom Allah has threatened with the Fire. This would lead to a strange contradiction and disorder in His speech, the Exalted. If it were said: "There is that which diverts these verses from including a single or double instance, such as the verses of repentance and intercession and their likes," then there is also that which diverts this verse from including the one who is God-conscious only once or twice, namely the very verses of threat for the destructive major sins, such as the verses regarding adultery, wrongful killing, usury, consuming the orphan's property, and their likes.

Moreover, the points he mentioned to approximate the indication are weak arguments, such as his saying: "This verse occurred after the saying of Iblis..." for you have already learned that this does not benefit him at all. And his saying: "Adding another restriction after faith is contrary to the principle..." for the principle is only relied upon in the absence of verbal evidence (الدليل اللفظي), and you have learned that there are numerous verses suitable for restriction (التقييد).

And his saying: "That is contrary to the literal sense," by which he seemingly means the literal sense of the absolute (المطلق) in its absolute application. He has overlooked that the literal sense of the absolute is only a proof when there is nothing suitable for restriction.

The truth is that the verse only includes those in whom the trait (ملكة) of godwariness has become established, which is refraining from the prohibitions of Allah. Those are the ones for whom happiness and Paradise are decreed by a necessary decree. Indeed, what is benefited from the Book and the Sunnah is that the people of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — who attend the Station with the testimony that there is no god but Allah, will not remain forever in the Fire and will inevitably enter Paradise. But this is different from the indication of the verse of the God-conscious regarding that.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِي صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ إِخْوَانًا عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مُّتَقَابِلِينَ** — **"We will remove whatever rancour there is in their breasts; [they will be] brothers, [reclining] on couches, facing one another"** (Al-Hijr: 47) until the end of the two verses. Rancour (الغل) is malice (الحقد). It is said to be the malice and envy in the breast that drives a person to harm others. Couches (السرر) is the plural of *sarir*. Fatigue (النصب) is the tiredness and exhaustion that comes from outside.

In these two verses, the Exalted describes the state of the God-conscious in their happiness upon entering Paradise. He specifically mentioned these matters among the blessings of Paradise, despite their abundance, because the concern necessitated by the context is related to explaining that they are in peace (سلام) and safety (أمن) from what the perverse were afflicted with—the nullification of happiness and the loss of sovereignty and dignity. Thus, He mentioned that they are in safety from within themselves, because Allah removed whatever rancour was in their breasts, so none of them intends evil for his companion; rather, they are brothers on couches facing one another. Their "facing one another" has a meaning that will come in the narrational discussion, if Allah wills. They are in safety from the side of external causes and factors, so no fatigue touches them at all. And they are in safety and peace from the side of their Lord, for they shall never be expelled from Paradise.
